planting.note: Plant bare-root or container-grown shrubs in spring; plant at least two different clones (not two of the same named cultivar) for good pollination and maximum fruit set.

directions: Plant in moist, fertile, slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; American elderberry is one of the most productive native fruiting shrubs, bearing enormous flat-topped flower clusters in early summer that are irresistible to pollinators, followed by drooping clusters of small dark purple-black berries in late summer used for syrups, jellies, wine, and immune-support preparations. Plant at least two genetically distinct clones for better cross-pollination and heavier fruit set. The shrub spreads by root suckers to form large thickets; remove suckers promptly to control spread. All parts of the plant except fully ripe, cooked berries and flowers should be considered toxic when consumed raw. Cut all canes to 12 inches in late winter every 3–4 years to rejuvenate; older canes are pithy and produce poor crops.

planting.note: Plant container-grown shrubs in spring in consistently moist to wet, acidic soil; do not allow to dry out during establishment. Do not plant too deeply — keep the crown at or above soil level.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, deeply acidic (pH 4.5–5.5), humus-rich soil in partial shade to light sun; swamp azalea is the most fragrant of all native North American deciduous azaleas, producing intensely clove-spice-scented white flowers (often pink-flushed) in late June through July, extending the native azalea bloom season well into summer after all other species have finished. It grows naturally in swamps, bogs, stream banks, and wet woodland margins from Maine to Florida, and tolerates standing water better than any other Rhododendron. The plant spreads slowly by rhizome and root sprouts to form a colony; purchase container-grown propagated plants only. Mulch with pine bark or pine needle litter to maintain soil acidity and moisture. Never allow to dry out in summer.

planting.note: Plant divisions in consistently moist to wet soil in partial shade; this plant requires reliable moisture and will stress in drought. Blooms 2–3 weeks earlier than the white species (Chelone glabra).

directions: Plant in consistently moist to wet, fertile, slightly acidic soil in partial shade to full sun; rose turtlehead produces deep rose-pink inflated flowers from midsummer into fall, blooming 2–3 weeks earlier than the similar white turtlehead and offering richer color. It grows naturally along stream banks, wet meadow margins, and boggy woodland edges from Maryland south into the Southeast. Bumblebees that are heavy enough to force open the closed flower mouth are its primary pollinators. Plant in groups of three or more for best visual impact. Divide clumps every 4 years in spring to maintain vigor; cut to the ground after hard frost. Excellent in rain gardens and wet borders where the reliable summer moisture it needs is naturally available.

planting.note: Direct sow at the soil surface in spring; seeds need light to germinate so do not cover. Can also start indoors 6–8 weeks before last frost. Self-sows reliably once established.

directions: Surface sow seed in moist, well-drained, average to slightly acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; purple giant hyssop is a tall, architecturally striking native mint family perennial of eastern woodland edges and clearings with lilac-purple flower spikes in midsummer to fall that are outstanding nectar sources for hummingbirds, sphinx moths, and a wide variety of native bees. The anise-scented foliage deters deer. It tolerates light shade well and performs better in partial shade in hot zone 7–8 summers than in full sun. Short-lived as a perennial but self-sows abundantly to maintain a naturalized colony; thin seedlings in spring. Excellent in native border plantings, prairie-edge gardens, and pollinator gardens.

planting.note: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep and 4–6 inches apart in groups of 10 or more in fall; the bulbs need a cold period to bloom. Avoid planting in wet soils that stay saturated in summer when bulbs are dormant.

directions: Plant bulbs 3–4 inches deep in fall in moist, well-drained, slightly acidic to neutral soil in partial shade to full sun; wild hyacinth is the smaller, more shade-tolerant of the eastern native camas species, growing naturally in moist prairie remnants, open woodland edges, and meadows from Ontario south to Texas. The pale blue-violet flowers on slender stems appear in mid-spring and are among the first native wildflowers to bloom, providing pollen for early native bees before most flowers have opened. Plant in large drifts (25+ bulbs) for the best visual effect. The foliage dies back completely by early summer, so mark plant locations and interplant with later-emerging perennials to fill the gap. The bulbs are edible — they were an important food source for Native American peoples — but require proper preparation.
